title: Optimisation and Precision Tuning of Localised Surface Plasmon Resonance in AuFON Systems
authors: Luis Alfonso Guerra Hernández, Osmar Gil Salas, Jorge Enrique Rueda Parada, Alejandro Fainstein, Andrés Alejandro Reynoso
abstract: Metal film on nanosphere (MFON) plasmonic systems have emerged as nanostructures with useful properties for molecular detection. This work presents the optimisation and discussion of the characterisation results of Au-film on nanosphere (AuFON) systems. Through experiments and numerical simulations, the resonance energies of localised surface plasmon modes and the spatial distribution of light on the nanostructured surface were identified. The results highlight the dependence of these modes on changes in nanostructure dimensions and the optical conditions of the incident radiation, thereby optimising the amplification of optical signals for applications in molecular detection.
summary: This work characterizes and optimizes the plasmonic properties of Au-film on nanosphere (AuFON) systems using a combination of advanced optical measurements and 3D electromagnetic simulations. By systematically varying parameters like nanosphere size and light polarization, the authors demonstrate precise control over the LSPR resonance energy. This tunability makes the AuFON platform highly promising for developing sensitive optical sensors for molecular detection.
main_problem: The paper aims to optimize and precisely tune the Localised Surface Plasmon Resonance (LSPR) in Gold-film on nanosphere (AuFON) systems to enhance optical signal amplification for molecular detection.
main_result: The LSPR resonance energy is highly dependent on the nanosphere diameter and polarization, with the LSPR2 mode showing the highest electric field intensification capacity for sensing applications.
method: The study combines experimental characterization using variable-angle spectroscopic ellipsometry with detailed numerical simulations solving Maxwell's equations in 3D using the finite element method.
model_or_system: The physical system is Au-film deposited on polystyrene nanosphere arrays (AuFON). The analysis involves modeling the interaction of incident light (varying in angle, polarization, and wavelength) with the plasmonic nanostructure.
key_observables: Resonance energies of LSPR modes (LSPR1 and LSPR2), spectral reflectivity response, and the spatial distribution/amplitude of the electric field.
important_parameters: Nanosphere diameter (500-900 nm), metal film thickness ($\approx 140$ nm), angle of incidence ($	heta$), and incident light polarization (TM/TE).
main_assumptions: The simulation relies on the dielectric function provided by Johnson and Christy, and the analysis assumes good ordering of the nanosphere array.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the spectral response (reflectivity) for varying diameters and polarizations, and show the dependence of resonance energy on the azimuthal angle ($\phi$) and the angle of incidence ($	heta$).
paper_structure: The paper systematically characterizes the LSPR modes by varying key parameters (diameter, angle, polarization) through both simulation and experiment, culminating in an optimized understanding of the system's tunability for sensing.
why_interesting: While focused on classical electromagnetism, the strong field enhancement and resonance phenomena are foundational to understanding light-matter interaction in nanoscale systems, which has parallels in cavity QED and quantum optics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. (a) Schematic of the optical reflectivity, where the incident light interacts with an AuFON nanostructured system. (b) High-resolution SEM image of a typical AuFON nanostructure with polystyrene nanospheres of 500 nm in diameter. (c) Unit cell of the simulated nanostructured system. The plane wave with TM or TE polarisation is incident on the metallic nanostructure. The incidence (θ) and azimuthal (ϕ) angles can be varied in the simulation, as well as the dimensions of the nanostructure.

Fig. 2. Simulation and experimental results of the spectral response of localised plasmonic modes in AuFON systems for TM (top) and TE (bottom) polarisations; in (a) for a 600 nm diameter sample and in (b) for a 500 nm diameter sample. The incidence angle (θ) was 25°, and the azimuthal angle (ϕ) was 0°.

Fig. 3. Simulation of the plasmonic response for a 500 nm diameter AuFON sample, for TM polarisation (panel (a)) and TE polarisation (panel (b)). The incidence angle was fixed at θ=25°, and ϕ was varied from 0° to 90° in 10° increments. The curves are vertically shifted by 0.2 for clarity. Fig. 4. Experimental results of the plasmonic response for a 500 nm diameter AuFON sample, for TM polarisation (panel (a)) and TE polarisation (panel (b)). The angle of incidence was fixed at 25°, and ϕ was varied from 0° to 90°. Each reflectivity curve was shifted vertically for clarity. Fig. 5. Simulated spectral response for a 500 nm diameter AuFON sample with different values of θ. (a) Optical reflectivity, and (b) the associated average electric field amplitude. For these results, ϕ=25° and TE polarisation were used. The curves are vertically shifted by 0.3 for clarity.
